label: foundation of
description: A Taylor series is built from repeated differentiation — the nth term is the nth derivative evaluated at the expansion point, so mastery of differentiation rules is a prerequisite for constructing any Taylor approximation.

label: applied in
description: The Greeks are partial derivatives of the option price with respect to its inputs — computing Delta, Gamma, Theta, and Vega requires direct application of the chain rule, product rule, and differentiation of the normal CDF.

label: used in
description: The Taylor expansion is itself a lemma — a proven result invoked as a building block inside larger proofs, such as deriving the Greeks or the Itô correction term.

label: exemplified by
description: Itô's Lemma is the most important lemma in quantitative finance — a proven stepping-stone result that is invoked every time a function of a stochastic process needs to be differentiated.

label: proves
description: The variance addition lemma for independent random variables — Var(X + Y) = Var(X) + Var(Y) — is the foundational step behind the square-root rule for scaling volatility.

label: motivates
description: Itô's Lemma is the stochastic calculus equivalent of a Taylor expansion — it tells you how a function of a random process changes, using the same first- and second-derivative structure.

label: extends
description: An American option is worth at least as much as its European counterpart — the early-exercise premium is the additional value from the right to exercise before expiry.

label: requires
description: Pricing an American option requires evaluating the early exercise decision at every node — the option price is the maximum of the intrinsic value and the continuation value.

label: compares
description: The comparison card contrasts the pricing methodologies, exercise rights, and put-call parity relationships that distinguish American from European options.

label: compares
description: The comparison card highlights that European options admit closed-form Black-Scholes prices and exact put-call parity, serving as the analytically tractable baseline.

label: constrains
description: Put-call parity holds exactly for European options but only as inequality bounds for American options — the early-exercise right breaks the strict equality.

label: informed by
description: Theta and the cost of carry determine whether early exercise is worthwhile — when Theta decay exceeds the interest earned on the intrinsic value, continuing to hold is optimal.

label: driven by
description: The core trade-off in early exercise is whether receiving cash now and earning interest outweighs the option's remaining time value — a direct application of the time value of money.

label: prices
description: The Black-Scholes formula gives closed-form prices for European calls and puts — it is the standard analytical pricing tool for European-style options.

label: valued via
description: European option prices equal the discounted expected payoff under the risk-neutral measure — this expectation is the defining calculation for European-style derivatives.

label: enables
description: Black-Scholes shows that a continuously rebalanced Delta hedge eliminates all price risk, which is precisely the argument used to derive the Black-Scholes PDE.

label: inverted to get
description: Implied volatility is found by running Black-Scholes in reverse: given a market price for an option, you solve numerically for the volatility that makes the formula match.

label: defines
description: The Black-Scholes formula gives closed-form expressions for Delta, Gamma, Vega, Theta and Rho — the Greeks are simply partial derivatives of the Black-Scholes price with respect to each input.

label: extends
description: The Heston model generalises Black-Scholes by replacing the constant-volatility assumption with a stochastic variance process — Black-Scholes is recovered as the special case where the vol of vol is zero.

label: explains
description: The Heston model's stochastic volatility and negative correlation between returns and variance reproduce the implied volatility smile and skew that Black-Scholes' flat volatility assumption cannot capture.

label: generalises
description: GBM assumes constant volatility, while the Heston model replaces this with a mean-reverting stochastic variance process — the asset price SDE is augmented with a second coupled SDE for the variance.

label: priced via
description: Although the Heston model has a semi-analytical solution for European options, exotic and path-dependent derivatives under Heston are typically priced by Monte Carlo simulation of the two correlated SDEs.

label: reveals
description: The volatility smile and skew observed in implied volatility across strikes expose the failure of Black-Scholes' constant-volatility assumption — the market prices in fat tails and jump risk that the model ignores.

label: derived via
description: The Greeks are partial derivatives of the option price; a Taylor expansion in those derivatives gives a local approximation of how the portfolio P&L changes with small moves in price, time, and volatility.

label: parameterises
description: Delta is the hedge ratio — it tells you exactly how many units of the underlying to hold in order to offset the option's directional exposure.

label: constrains
description: Put-call parity forces the Delta of a put and the Delta of the equivalent call to sum to one (or negative one), providing a quick sanity check on any Greeks calculation.

label: framework for
description: The Black-Scholes price equals the discounted expected payoff under the risk-neutral measure — risk-neutral pricing is the theoretical foundation that justifies the formula.

label: input to
description: Daily returns calculated from asset prices are the raw data whose mean and standard deviation characterise the asset's drift and volatility.

label: generalised by
description: The time value of money uses a single discount rate, but the yield curve generalises this to a different rate for every maturity — each cash flow is discounted at its own spot rate rather than one flat rate.

label: analysed via
description: PCA on the covariance matrix of yield curve changes decomposes movements into eigenvectors (level, slope, curvature) — typically three factors explain over 95% of all variation in interest rates.

label: bootstrapped via
description: Constructing the yield curve from coupon bond prices requires solving a triangular system of linear equations — each new maturity adds one equation and one unknown spot rate.

label: requires
description: The Cholesky decomposition A = LLᵀ is only defined for symmetric, positive-definite matrices — the symmetry of the covariance matrix is a prerequisite for the factorisation.

label: enables
description: Multi-asset Monte Carlo simulation uses the Cholesky decomposition of the correlation matrix to convert independent random draws into correctly correlated shocks — without it, simulated paths would ignore diversification.

label: decomposes
description: The Cholesky factorisation splits the correlation matrix into a lower triangular matrix and its transpose — this is the practical mechanism for imposing a correlation structure on simulated random variables.

label: gates
description: A matrix is invertible if and only if its determinant is non-zero — checking the determinant is the first step before attempting to invert a covariance matrix for portfolio optimisation.

label: product gives
description: The determinant of a matrix equals the product of its eigenvalues — a zero eigenvalue means the matrix is singular, directly linking spectral analysis to invertibility.

label: simplified by
description: Symmetric matrices guarantee real eigenvalues and orthogonal eigenvectors — this is why covariance matrices, which are always symmetric, admit clean PCA decompositions with interpretable risk factors.

label: decomposes
description: Each eigenvalue of the covariance matrix represents the variance explained by its corresponding eigenvector — the total variance is the sum of all eigenvalues, partitioning risk across independent factors.

label: solves
description: The solution to Ax = b is x = A⁻¹b — computing the matrix inverse is one method for solving systems of linear equations, used in regression and portfolio optimisation.

label: prerequisite for
description: Computing the determinant of larger matrices via cofactor expansion requires repeated multiplication of matrix elements — matrix arithmetic is the foundation on which the determinant is built.

label: defines
description: The inverse is defined by the product AA⁻¹ = I — verifying and computing an inverse requires matrix multiplication.

label: used in
description: Portfolio VaR for multiple assets requires the matrix calculation w'Σw to compute portfolio variance — matrix multiplication is the computational tool that combines individual volatilities with their correlations.

label: computes
description: The correlation matrix is built and manipulated via matrix operations — portfolio risk, principal component analysis, and factor models all rely on multiplying weight vectors by covariance or correlation matrices.

label: checked via
description: The determinant determines whether a system has a unique solution (det ≠ 0), no solution, or infinitely many solutions — it is the first diagnostic tool for any linear system.

label: used in
description: The portfolio variance formula wᵀΣw requires the transpose operation to convert the weight vector into a row — the transpose is integral to every quadratic form in matrix algebra.

label: updates
description: Bayes' Theorem updates your probability distribution as new information arrives; the expected value computed from that updated distribution is your revised estimate of fair value.

label: prices via
description: The binomial tree model prices American options by working backwards through a recombining tree — at each node the number of up-moves follows a binomial distribution, and early exercise is checked at every step.

label: limiting case of
description: When the number of trials n is large and the probability p is small, the binomial distribution converges to the Poisson — this limit is used in credit risk to model rare default events in large portfolios.

label: defines mean of
description: The expected value of a binomial random variable is np — this simple formula gives the average number of up-moves in a binomial tree, which determines the expected terminal stock price.

label: converges to
description: By the Central Limit Theorem, the binomial distribution approaches the normal distribution as n grows — this convergence is why the binomial tree model reproduces Black-Scholes prices in the continuous limit.

label: illustrates
description: The binomial distribution is the textbook illustration of the CLT — the sum of n independent Bernoulli trials becomes approximately normal as n grows, which is exactly the convergence that turns a binomial tree into Black-Scholes.

label: counts steps of
description: Each step of a discrete random walk is an independent up-or-down trial — the total number of up-moves after n steps follows a binomial distribution B(n, p).

label: built from
description: The chi-squared distribution is the sum of squared standard normals — it arises naturally when testing whether the variance of normally distributed returns has changed.

label: component of
description: The t-distribution is constructed as a standard normal divided by the square root of a chi-squared divided by its degrees of freedom — the two distributions are algebraically inseparable in small-sample inference.

label: tests
description: The chi-squared test statistic (n−1)s²/σ₀² follows a chi-squared distribution under the null hypothesis — this is the standard method for testing whether realised volatility matches a hypothesised value.

label: applied in
description: The Black-Scholes formula uses Φ(d₁) and Φ(d₂) — the cumulative normal distribution function — to compute the option price as a weighted combination of probabilities.

label: integrates
description: The CDF is the integral of the PDF — F(x) accumulates the density from −∞ to x, and conversely the PDF is the derivative of the CDF.

label: inverted via
description: The inverse CDF (quantile function) transforms a uniform U(0,1) draw into a sample from any target distribution — this inverse transform method is the bridge between uniform random numbers and Monte Carlo simulation.

label: defines quantile of
description: Value at Risk is defined as a quantile of the loss distribution — it is the inverse CDF evaluated at the confidence level, making the CDF the mathematical object that directly determines VaR.

label: inter-arrival times of
description: If events arrive as a Poisson process with rate λ, the waiting time between consecutive events follows an exponential distribution with the same rate — the two distributions are inseparable.

label: mirrors
description: The survival probability e^{−λt} has the same exponential form as the continuous discount factor e^{−rt} — default intensity in credit risk plays an analogous role to the risk-free rate in time-value-of-money discounting.

label: discounted by
description: A fair price equals the expected future payoff discounted back to today — combining expected value with time-value-of-money discounting is the core of risk-neutral option pricing.

label: addresses
description: Jump-diffusion models add Poisson-distributed jumps to GBM to capture the fat tails and sudden crashes that pure Brownian motion cannot explain — a direct response to the limitations of the normal model.

label: augments
description: In Merton's jump-diffusion model, a Poisson process is layered on top of Brownian motion — the continuous diffusion handles normal price moves while Poisson jumps capture sudden large moves.

label: computes
description: The expected value of a continuous random variable is defined as the integral of x weighted by the PDF — E[X] = ∫x f(x) dx — making the density function the essential ingredient for computing fair values.

label: exemplified by
description: The normal distribution's bell-shaped PDF is the most widely used density in finance — its specific functional form determines the probabilities that feed into Black-Scholes, VaR, and every Gaussian risk model.

label: generalises
description: The t-distribution has heavier tails than the normal and converges to it as degrees of freedom increase — with low ν it captures the excess kurtosis observed in real asset returns.

label: improves
description: Using the t-distribution instead of the normal for VaR calculations produces wider tail quantiles, giving more conservative and realistic loss estimates that account for fat tails in returns.

label: drives
description: Monte Carlo simulation begins with uniform U(0,1) random draws, which are transformed into the target distribution via the inverse CDF method — the uniform distribution is the raw input to every simulation.

label: assumed by
description: Black-Scholes assumes stock prices are lognormally distributed — the N(d₁) and N(d₂) terms in the formula are computed from the normal distribution of log-prices.

label: N(d1), N(d2) from
description: The Black-Scholes formula contains N(d₁) and N(d₂), which are cumulative normal probabilities representing the option's Delta and the risk-neutral probability of finishing in the money.

label: exponentiates to
description: If log-returns are normally distributed then the price itself is lognormally distributed — the lognormal is simply the exponential of a normal random variable.

label: used in
description: Parametric VaR assumes returns are normally distributed, so the 1% or 5% loss quantile is simply the mean minus a fixed multiple of the standard deviation (1.645σ for 95%, 2.326σ for 99%).

label: fits
description: Standardised daily returns closely match the normal bell curve — this empirical observation is the justification for modelling returns as normally distributed random variables.

label: parameterised by
description: The normal distribution is fully described by its mean and variance — knowing these two parameters is enough to calculate any probability or quantile, including VaR.

label: increments of
description: Brownian motion is built from normal increments — each infinitesimal step dW is drawn from N(0, dt), so the normal distribution is the elementary building block of all continuous-time models.

label: challenges
description: Real returns have fatter tails than the normal distribution predicts — extreme events like the 1987 crash occur far more often than the model allows, undermining VaR and Black-Scholes in the tails.

label: questions
description: The basic model assumes constant drift and volatility, but real markets exhibit volatility clustering and regime changes — motivating GARCH, stochastic volatility, and jump-diffusion models.

label: computes
description: Monte Carlo VaR simulates thousands of portfolio paths and reads the loss quantile directly from the resulting distribution, avoiding the normality assumption of parametric VaR.

label: converges to
description: The CLT states that the standardised sum of i.i.d. random variables converges to the normal distribution — this is the fundamental reason the normal distribution is the default model for aggregate returns and portfolio losses.

label: justifies
description: Parametric VaR assumes portfolio losses are normally distributed — the CLT justifies this by guaranteeing that the sum of many independent risk factors is approximately normal, even when individual factors are not.

label: validates
description: The CLT guarantees that sample means are approximately normal for large n, which justifies constructing confidence intervals and hypothesis tests for estimated drift and volatility using normal quantiles.

label: feeds into
description: Portfolio VaR depends on the correlations between positions — in a multi-asset portfolio, low correlations reduce aggregate risk even when individual volatilities are unchanged.

label: motivates
description: Empirical return distributions exhibit fat tails, skewness, and volatility clustering that deviate from the normal assumption — these observed properties are the primary evidence motivating more sophisticated risk models.

label: calibrates
description: The annualised drift and volatility used in every pricing and risk model are estimated from historical daily returns — multiplying the mean by 252 and the standard deviation by √252.

label: contrasted in
description: Mean returns scale linearly with time while volatility scales with the square root — this asymmetry explains why drift dominates at long horizons but is invisible at short ones.

label: scales
description: The square-root rule is used to convert VaR between time horizons — a 1-day VaR is scaled to a 10-day VaR by multiplying by √10, as required by Basel regulatory standards.

label: explains
description: Because volatility grows as √t while drift grows as t, the signal-to-noise ratio improves with horizon length — the square-root rule is the quantitative reason why short-term forecasting is so difficult.

label: underpins
description: The square-root rule follows directly from the variance addition property for independent random variables — total variance equals the sum of individual variances, so total standard deviation grows as √n.

label: example of
description: Standard Brownian motion is the canonical continuous-time martingale — its independent, zero-mean increments ensure that the conditional expectation of any future value equals the current value.

label: underlies
description: Because the underlying asset follows a Brownian-driven process, option prices are stochastic and their sensitivities (the Greeks) describe how that randomness flows through to the portfolio.

label: extended to
description: Geometric Brownian Motion replaces the additive increments of standard Brownian Motion with multiplicative ones, ensuring the stock price can never go negative.

label: foundation of
description: Risk-neutral pricing rests on the martingale property — under the risk-neutral measure Q, discounted asset prices are martingales, which is the mathematical condition that guarantees no-arbitrage pricing.

label: defined via
description: The martingale property is a statement about conditional expected values — E[M_t | F_s] = M_s — so understanding expectation is a prerequisite for understanding martingales.

label: becomes under Q
description: Under the real-world measure, GBM with drift μ ≠ r is not a martingale after discounting — but under the risk-neutral measure Q, the discounted GBM stock price becomes a martingale, which is the key insight enabling derivative pricing.

label: implements
description: Monte Carlo simulation works by repeatedly applying the discrete random walk formula with fresh random draws — each application of the formula generates one step of one simulated price path.

label: becomes
description: The discrete random walk is the finite-step approximation that becomes the continuous SDE dS = μS dt + σS dX as the time step δt → 0.

label: models stock price for
description: Black-Scholes assumes the stock price follows GBM — this single assumption leads directly to the lognormal distribution of returns and the closed-form pricing formula.

label: produces
description: GBM implies that log-prices are normally distributed, so the price itself follows a lognormal distribution — this is why asset prices are always positive and right-skewed.

label: derives
description: Applying Itô's Lemma to the option price as a function of a GBM stock price, then invoking the no-arbitrage condition, yields the Black-Scholes PDE.

label: solves
description: Itô's Lemma is used to derive the explicit solution for GBM — applying it to ln(S) converts the stochastic differential equation into a simple integral that gives S(t) in closed form.

label: foundation of
description: The SDE dS = μS dt + σS dX is the starting point for deriving the Black-Scholes PDE — applying Itô's Lemma and the no-arbitrage condition to this equation produces the pricing formula.

label: parameterised by
description: The SDE contains exactly two parameters — drift μ and volatility σ — which fully specify the probabilistic behaviour of the asset price.

label: improves tail of
description: EVT provides a statistically rigorous estimate of VaR at very high confidence levels (99.9%) by modelling the tail directly, rather than relying on the normal approximation that systematically underestimates extreme losses.

label: addresses
description: EVT directly confronts the fat-tail limitation of Gaussian risk models — by fitting only the extreme observations, it captures the heavy tails that the normal distribution misses.

label: extends beyond
description: The normal distribution's exponentially decaying tails correspond to the Gumbel case (ξ = 0) of the GEV family — financial returns typically exhibit Fréchet-type tails (ξ > 0), which are heavier than the normal allows.

label: motivates
description: The empirically observed fat tails, excess kurtosis, and occasional extreme moves in financial returns are precisely the phenomena that EVT is designed to model — stylised facts of returns provide the motivation for using EVT.

label: critiques
description: VaR violates the subadditivity axiom of coherence — merging portfolios can increase VaR, penalising diversification — which is the central motivation for replacing VaR with coherent alternatives like Expected Shortfall.

label: complements
description: EVT models the shape of the tail while coherence axioms govern how a risk measure aggregates tail losses — combining EVT-fitted tails with a coherent measure like Expected Shortfall produces robust extreme-risk estimates.

label: formalises
description: The coherence axioms formalise the shortcomings of naive risk measures — subadditivity failure in VaR is a specific, mathematically precise model limitation that coherent measures are designed to eliminate.

label: defined via
description: Both VaR and Expected Shortfall are defined as functionals of the loss CDF — VaR is the quantile (inverse CDF) and ES is the average of quantiles beyond the VaR level, so the CDF is the mathematical foundation for evaluating coherence.

label: depends on
description: Portfolio variance is determined by the pairwise correlations between assets — the off-diagonal terms of the covariance matrix drive the diversification benefit that is the central result of MPT.

label: minimises
description: MPT defines risk as portfolio variance and seeks the weight vector that minimises it for a given target return — variance is the objective function at the heart of mean-variance optimisation.

label: computed via
description: Portfolio variance is the quadratic form w'Σw and expected return is the dot product w'μ — both are matrix multiplications, making linear algebra the computational engine of portfolio optimisation.

label: feeds into
description: The portfolio variance from MPT is the key input to parametric VaR — once mean-variance weights are chosen, the portfolio standard deviation is plugged directly into the VaR formula to quantify downside risk.

label: appears in
description: The diffusion coefficient σS enters the Black-Scholes PDE as the ½σ²S² term multiplying the second derivative — it is the source of the Gamma risk that delta hedging must manage.

label: scales by
description: The diffusion coefficient σS combines the volatility parameter σ with the current price S — volatility sets the percentage scale of randomness, while the diffusion coefficient converts it into absolute price units.

label: decomposed into
description: The SDE dS = μS dt + σS dW splits into a drift term and a diffusion term — the diffusion coefficient b(S) = σS is the function that controls the size of the random component.

label: multiplies
description: The diffusion coefficient scales the Brownian increment dW — without it, the raw Wiener process has unit variance per unit time, so the diffusion coefficient is what calibrates the noise to match observed market volatility.

label: decomposed into
description: The SDE dS = μS dt + σS dW splits into a drift term and a diffusion term — the drift coefficient a(S) = μS is the function that controls the expected rate of change.

label: formalises
description: The drift coefficient a(S) = μS is the continuous-time formalisation of the drift parameter μ — it converts the annualised expected return into an instantaneous rate of price change proportional to the current level.

label: replaced under
description: Under the risk-neutral measure the drift coefficient changes from μS to rS — this replacement is the mathematical content of risk-neutral pricing and the reason option prices do not depend on expected returns.

label: paired with
description: The drift and diffusion coefficients are the two components of any SDE — the drift controls the deterministic trend while the diffusion controls the random fluctuations, and together they fully specify the process.

label: driven by
description: The diffusion coefficient b(X) enters the Fokker-Planck equation as the second-order term ½∂²(b²p)/∂x² — it determines how quickly the probability density spreads out over time.

label: driven by
description: The drift coefficient a(X) enters the Fokker-Planck equation as the first-order term −∂(ap)/∂x — it determines the direction in which the probability density shifts over time.

label: dual of
description: The Fokker-Planck equation and the Black-Scholes PDE are adjoint operators — Fokker-Planck evolves the density forward in time while Black-Scholes evolves the option price backward, both governed by the same drift and diffusion.

label: derives
description: Solving the Fokker-Planck equation for GBM confirms that the terminal stock price is lognormally distributed — the equation provides the rigorous proof of the distributional assumption underlying Black-Scholes.

label: maps across strikes
description: The volatility smile is the graph of implied volatility as a function of strike price — each point on the smile is an implied volatility extracted from a single option's market price via the Black-Scholes formula.

label: exposes limitation of
description: The existence of the smile directly contradicts Black-Scholes' constant-volatility assumption — if the model were exact, implied volatility would be flat across all strikes.

label: reproduced by
description: The Heston model's stochastic volatility and correlation between returns and variance generate an implied volatility smile from first principles, providing a theoretically grounded alternative to strike-by-strike fitting.

label: evidences
description: The volatility smile is empirical proof that real return distributions have fatter tails and asymmetry compared to the lognormal — it is one of the most visible model limitations in practice.

label: modifies
description: Mean reversion adds a restoring drift term κ(θ − X) to the Brownian motion framework — without it, Brownian motion drifts without bound, which is unsuitable for quantities like interest rates.

label: state-dependent
description: In the Ornstein-Uhlenbeck process the drift coefficient is κ(θ − X), which depends on the current state — when X is above θ the drift is negative, pulling the process back down, and vice versa.

label: models rates for
description: Mean-reverting models like Vasicek and Hull-White are the standard tools for modelling the short rate — the mean reversion ensures that interest rates stay within realistic bounds and the yield curve can be fitted exactly.

label: uses
description: The Heston model's variance process is a mean-reverting square-root diffusion (CIR process) — the speed of mean reversion κ and long-run variance θ control how quickly volatility returns to its equilibrium after a shock.
